title: Waxing bookbinding thread
subtitle: Drawing thread across beeswax before sewing to reduce friction and fraying
excerpt: Waxing thread is a two-minute step at the start of every sewing session that makes a measurable difference to the result. Wax stiffens the thread so it pulls through pierced holes cleanly, stops fraying at the needle eye, and reduces the friction that causes loose stitches in tight signatures.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on sewing materials and thread preparation. Internet Archive scan available. Cockerell specifies beeswax as the standard thread conditioner for bookbinding.

glossaryTerms.definition: Drawing thread across a block of beeswax so the wax coats the fibres, reducing friction and fraying during sewing.
Unravelling of the outer fibres of a thread at the needle eye, caused by repeated friction through a pierced hole.

content.text: Bookbinding thread comes pre-waxed in most cases, but drawing it across fresh beeswax before use adds a second coat that lasts the full sewing session. Un-waxed or under-waxed thread frays at the needle eye and tangles when you pull it through multiple sewing stations. Waxing takes less time than threading the needle.
Thread: waxed linen bookbinding thread, 4-cord. Cut a working length of no more than 80 cm: longer threads tangle and wear through wax as they pass repeatedly through the same hole.
Beeswax block: pure beeswax, not paraffin. Paraffin wax is harder and drier; it coats thread but does not reduce friction as well as the softer beeswax.
Hold the beeswax block in one hand. Lay the thread across the top face of the block.
Press down lightly with your thumb and draw the thread firmly along the full length in a single pass. The thread should leave a faint white groove in the wax surface.
Repeat on the reverse side of the thread.
Thread the needle and draw the full working length through your fingertips once. This friction heats and smooths the wax coating into the thread fibres.
at the needle eye: if the thread end is splitting, cut it back 5 mm and re-thread.
does not fix already-frayed thread; it prevents it.

attrs.symptom: Thread sticks together in a loop when pulled through a hole
attrs.cause: Too much wax applied; the thread has clumped.

content.text: One firm pass each side is enough. Wipe off excess wax with a dry cloth before sewing.

attrs.cause: The wax was applied to the thread before the end was cut cleanly; the frayed end was not trimmed.

content.text: Cut the thread end cleanly at 45 degrees before waxing. The diagonal cut produces a pointed tip that passes through the needle eye and the pierced hole cleanly.

Waxing is the first step in every bookbinding sewing session. Apply it before the kettle stitch, the Coptic stitch, and the French-link stitch. The wax also reduces the pull force needed on tight Japanese stab-binding holes, where un-waxed thread can tear the paper.
